title: The Change List
subtitle: {key:g;} / {key:g,} — walk through the places you've been editing.
summary: Like the jump list, but for *changes*: {key:g}{key:;} jumps back to your previous edit position; {key:g}{key:,} walks forward.

blocks.text: Every *change* you make sets a position in a per-buffer list. {key:g}{key:;} walks back through it; {key:g}{key:,} walks forward. Different from the jump list — changes only, this buffer only.

blocks.title: Change List vs Jump List
blocks.text:
**Jump list** — per-window. Records *positions you've been*, regardless of whether you edited there. Used for navigating with {key:Ctrl-O}/{key:Ctrl-I}.

**Change list** — per-buffer. Records *positions where you actually changed text*. Used for revisiting your edits with {key:g}{key:;}/{key:g}{key:,}.

The special mark {key:'}{key:.}/{key:`}{key:.} is just "the most-recent entry of this buffer's change list." {key:g}{key:;}{key:g}{key:;}{key:g}{key:;} walks deeper into history, two-then-three-then-four edits ago.

Especially useful when you've been editing in many places in one file and want to revisit each spot to verify.
Combined with {key:Ctrl-O}/{key:Ctrl-I}, you have two complementary trails: places you've *been* and places you've *changed*. Different muscle memory; both invaluable.

blocks.title: Change list is parallel to undo, not the same
blocks.text: Every modification stamps the cursor position into a separate change list. {key:g}{key:;} and {key:g}{key:,} navigate it without touching the buffer state. That separation is why 'go to where I last edited' works after you've undone, redone, or jumped away — the list records positions of edits, not snapshots of edits.
